turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in No. 89-1909, Feist Publications, Inc., v. Rural Telephone Service Company, Inc. Mr. Knobbe?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Knobbe, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Knobbe, excuse me.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case involves the copyrightability of a phone subscriber's name, address, and telephone number as published by the phone company in its directory. The effect of the decisions below, which came from the district of Kansas in the Tenth Circuit, is to hold that a private company cannot publish these facts in its own directory without first doing a canvas to obtain the facts from the phone subscriber. Our position is that while the telephone company's directory may be copyrightable as a whole, the phone company's alphabetical list of names, address, and telephone numbers of its subscribers is not a work of authorship under the statute, Section 101 of the copyright

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, good idea.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: To do that — what Feist did was went to the various phone companies here and licensed their updated white page listings so he could print those in the directory. Now, one thing that's important to note is he didn't necessarily just license the directory. He licensed updated, white page listings, the information that's available to the directory operator.. The reason for that is, for example, if you look on the map, the directory that covers the green area in the lower left-hand corner, that directory may have came in February. The directory that covers the red area up in the top may have came out in November. Now a Feist directory that covers the whole area comes out in December, then the listings from the red area that came out in November, those will be fairly current. But the listings from this green area down here, if your looking solely at the directory, are almost a year out of date.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Counsel, did Feist leave in its directory the listings broken down by the areas you're showing or did it recompile the list into one giant alphabetical list?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: It's in one giant alphabetical list, Your Honor. The two directories are part —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not broken down by the smaller areas?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Not broken down by town. If you wanted to call Joe Blow and you knew he lived in Jennings Kansas --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Uh-huh.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: — in Feist's directory you still looked under the B's till you got to Blow. .Now, if you wanted to call Joe Blow and you were sure of where he lived. He may have lived in Jennings or Decatur — you weren't sure — it's up in there somewhere — you could still go alphabetically to look for Blow. All of these companies licensed their listings to Feist except for the yellow area. That's Rural Telephone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did Feist's directory show the name of the city or the name of the town?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, Feist's directory shows the - maybe I misunderstand the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if you wanted to look up this person and you looked him up, could you see not only his phone number —

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That isn't what was done here, but you take the position —

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: No, it is not what was done here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — it could have been done.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: That's correct. But that's our position if you look at the — if you look at the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And somebody could do --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say it's not a compilation at all?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: No, they're all preexisting facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: It's simply an alphabetical list of their subscribers. It doesn't comply — the statute is Section 101 —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: — which defines a compilation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And this is not it? This is not a compilation?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: This is a compilation in the normal accepted term, but it's not a compilation as defined in the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: It's not a copyrightable compilation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So I assume that somebody could just come in and xerox your directory and sell it, right?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: If --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're in a pretty precarious business there.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: I think so. If you're looking for a handle, you know, maybe that's the handle. Maybe the handle is xerox piracy or something of the pages. But you can't protect the name, address, and telephone number in a simple alphabetical list.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, now, what's the statutory language that you rely on, please?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: The statutory language is in Section 101 of the copyright act. That defines a compilation as a work formed by the collection and assembly of preexisting materials or data that are selected, coordinated, or arranged in such a way that the resulting work as a whole constitutes an original work of authorship.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you say that an alphabetical listing of phone numbers cannot be an original work of authorship?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: It doesn't — it does — yes, I say that. It doesn't exhibit any coordination or arrangement. An alphabetical arrangement can't be a copyrightable thing. That's simply the most efficient way to arrange something. Wouldn't we have some severe problems with dictionary people and everyone else if you could copyright the alphabet?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about computer databases generally? Same thing.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Well — same thing, but computer databases I think are really — I don't know if they're published or not. I mean there's a lot of stuff in the amicus here about, oh, we're going to affect this — you know the sky will fall and nothing will be copyrightable. Most computer databases are handled by license agreements. They're protected by contract. I contract my computer database to you and as part of that your contract says you're not going to further disseminate it or whatever. Here we've got a published -- we've got a published directory.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I mean ,there is certainly some selection. If you're adverting to the words of the statute, there certainly has been some selection here, hasn't there, of names? Their directory selected only the names of people in the yellow areas.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: That's their entire list of subscribership.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it may well be, but that's a selection. They didn't publish anybody else's subscribership. That's a selectionship, isn't it?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: We don't think — publishing your entire list doesn't seem to be a selection to us.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, sure, I've just chosen to publish my entire list and not somebody else's list. Why isn't that selection?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Well —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I could have done what you did. They, they could have decided to put out a directory for the whole thing. They didn't. They said, we're going to be more selective. We're only going to publish a list of the people in our (inaudible). Now you may think that's not a very — what should I say — inventive selection, but it is a selection, is it not?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: If it's a selection, it's not a copyrightable selection. That's our position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, okay, so what it comes to, it has to be the kind of a selection that causes — that causes the whole thing to be a work of authorship.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: A work of authorship, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where do we look for that — for what kind of a selection causes something to be a work worth of authorship?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Isn't there some type of — our all the restaurants in the State. No, I'm going to be more selective. People aren't interested in all the restaurants in the State, just all the restaurants in the city. That's a selection. Now, why is that selection in your view not copyrightable. But if I say, because I know what your view is — if I say just the best restaurants in town, that is. Now why — what's the difference between those two?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: In our view, that has made some selection, other than just publish everything.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I just said —

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: If I publish —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn't publish everything. I'm just publishing the restaurants in this city. I've made the judgment that —

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: All are published are facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — a list of all the restaurants in the State would be useless to people. But I know a lot of people that would want to know all the restaurants in the State capital. So I'm being selective to that extent. That's a selection.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: What I've published then is I've selected just to publish all the facts, and all the facts that I've published cannot be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not all the facts.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: — are not protected.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just the restaurants in the State capital. I've excluded restaurants elsewhere. That's a selection, isn't it? You will not concede that that's a selection?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: I will concede that's a selection. I won't concede that that's a copyrightable selection, because what have I done other than just publish a list of the facts?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if that's true, the Encyclopedia Britannica just publishes facts. Can you copy that?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Their coordination, arrangement, selection, if they exhibit sufficient coordination, arrangement, and selection, sure that's copyrightable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why haven't you got it here? A variant on Justice Scalia's question is even within the service or the phone company, all the phone company publishes are the names and addresses and numbers of people who subscribe to phone service. They don't publish the listing of everyone who lives in that town. That's a selection. That's not publishing all the facts about the people in that town. It's publishing facts only about people who subscribe to phone service.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. Our position is seating chart of the Supreme Court. If I come in here and make this out and I select only to publish the senior- most five justices -- I mean, if I select to publish here's the seating chart, haven't I just published everything? I published all the facts. So I don't quite understand what work of authorship I've done. It's appears to me that the selection, coordination, and arrangement has to be like the baseball card case where the author selected the 5,000 premium cards out of there, the X case out of the Second Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't the difference that you can find out who the members of the Court are without having to consult that card. The fact it's available elsewhere? Whereas if we were a secret Court and the only way you could find our who we were would be by looking at a chart published by some third party, that chart would be copyrightable insofar as it was a compilation of the names, wouldn't it?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, that it's a public fact? I mean — yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay, well, in this case, you're not publishing facts which are generally available to you through sources that you use. You're simply publishing the compilation itself or a portion of the compilation as distinguished from the situation you would be in if you information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what does the — does the statute require more than just selection? What are the Register of Copyright's guidelines on this? Can a simply alphabetical listing of all the data in a given area meet the Register's guidelines for what meets Section 101?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: No, as I understand it —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do we have to look at here?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: As I understand it —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And does it have to result ultimately in an original work of authorship?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, maybe it's not enough to focus on the word "selection." Maybe the statute requires a series of things.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: There are three portions to the statute. Collecting and assembling, which is the sweat- of-the-brow theory that's adopted below, and that is if you put a lot of stuff together, you're an author of it. The second portion is selection, coordination, and arrangement. And the third is so that the work as a whole is a work of authorship.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: An original work of authorship?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, an original work of authorship under the statute, under 101. works of — works of authorship, and those are copyrightable. There are some — in this directory of the phone company's, there's forward text in here. This has how to — you know, how to dial the phone company, how to make a long distance call, a party line. I assume Rural authored that and that's copyrightable. But that's really not what's involved. What's involved in this case is this little piece right here, the alphabetical list of white pages. Yeah, it's a public domain fact. .The other statute is 103(b). 103(b) says that the copyright in a compilation, okay, extends only to the material contributed by the author as distinguished from preexisting material employed in the work and does not imply any exclusive right to that. For example, the decision of this Court are public domain information. As a — they're preexisting material. If, as a compiler, I put together all the decisions of this Court dealing with copyright law, then I've got a copyright in that as a whole, but I don't have a copyright in the decision of the Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Knobbe, do you think the 1976 act changed the copyrightability of a directory such as is involved here?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, the '76 act I assume is in the public domain. Once I -- once I make the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do you mean by that statement?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: It's available to the public. I have not paid the phone company to have an unlisted number, so I've allowed the phone company to give it to the directory information operator, to publish it. It's a preexisting fact. I have that name, address — that's my name, address, and telephone number. So it has to be public domain information. What else can it be?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I guess I'm not sure how you're using the term. You seem to be using the term to mean that if the subject matter of information consents to its publication, then anything which publishes that information is in the public domain. Is that what you mean?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: That — yes, that if it's a fact, it's in the public domain. Once we publish a fact, that's part of the public domain available to any later authors. With the Court's permission I'd like to reserve my remaining time for rebuttal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Knobbe. Mr. Caplinger, we'll hear now from you. contained in the white pages, can you?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: No. What is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So what is to prevent Feist from looking at your directory and taking the listings of names and telephone numbers and including them in their own —

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, the — Justice O'Connor —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — as factual material?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, we are not alleging that the copyright in the telephone directory extends to the names — to the particular name and particular number. What a copyright and a compilation extends to is the — is to the work of authorship on the part of the author.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what Feist did was to take the listings, names and telephone numbers, and use them in their own directory.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, and Justice O'Connor, I'm saying that the white page listings are not preexisting material. The name by itself. The address by itself. But without the telephone company being author of compiling and creating a listing — when I say listing it means all here —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, didn't the telephone company, Rural, simply make an alphabetical listing of all telephone subscribers within its geographical area covered by the book?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: That --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that what was done?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, that was one part of the selection. I think that to -- to fully answer your question, let's look right at 103, the definition of a compilation. And a compilation is a work formed by the collection and the assembly of preexisting materials or of data that are selected, coordinated, or arranged in such a way that the resulting work as a whole constitutes an original work of authorship. What Rural Telephone — just like any other telephone company — they on a day-to-day basis gather, collect, and assemble a lot of information about the subscriber. The addresses, the towns, the types of equipment, et cetera, et cetera. There's a lot of information they receive. Then what they do is then they turn and they make the subjective decision of how to select, how to coordinate, and how to arrange that and how to create something that's a useable —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't it just an alphabetical listing by geographic area?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: In this particular area, yeah, that was the decision to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that what you have there?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- what elements of originality have you added to those facts that make this a work of original authorship under 101?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, Justice Kennedy, I think that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You've said — you've told us one of the things, and that is you've selected the counties in this yellow area. What else?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: We've —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You've alphabetized, which I think we could talk about, but I don't think that's very original. What else?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: We decided to, instead of — we decided to put them in one list instead of 26 telephone exchanges.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that just repeats what you said before. All right.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Communities. We decided to include business listings within this. We decided that in some communities we only put the town where in other communities we put the full address. We also decide what name, because my full name might be a fact. But I go by several names, James, Jim, Jay, JR, et cetera. And —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not stunned by the originality so far. lower courts. I don't see a split in the lower courts. What you have is this — supposedly this sweat-of-the- brow theory on one side, and the original act of authorship on the other side. The original or the sweat of the brow is where the author gathers, collects, and assembles. He gathers all this factual information. But if he doesn't then somehow make a decision on how to put that in a compilation, it's not — it's not a useable compilation. You have to make — after you collect this the sweat of the brow and you have all —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He has to say, ah-hah, I am going to put this in there alphabetically.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He has to make that further step.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Scalia, that is — that is one step.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, my, my, that's —

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: At —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You think — it seems to me that we have here a statutory definition that is obviously a definition of art. If you wanted the common meaning of compilation, you would have stopped that definition right after the first phrase. It would read a compilation is a whole constitutes an original work of authorship. And we're being asked in this case to give content to what that means: "in such a way that the resulting work constitutes an original work of authorship."

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what — original — you —

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Scalia —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — disclaim any inventiveness or novelty or anything else in that?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Scalia, the — I know that alphabetizing this list doesn't take a lot of thought, but there are other things that went into this and in fact if it wasn't for Rural Telephone being the author of this, this information would not exist. Yes, the names, addresses, and numbers are floating out there in bits and pieces, but they could still be floating until Rural Telephone took that information, decided — selected what it was going to include in the directory, how it was going to coordinate that directory, and how it was going to arrange that information. And just because it is — it appears to be simple, it still meets the test under the 1976 act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the fact that it wouldn't be available proves not — I mean you can say that of any collection and assembling of preexisting materials. Your doing the same. Whereas in patent law, if Feist invented something at the same time that Rural invented it. Whoever finished inventing that first has a monopoly on it forever. But in copyright law, if they both go out and independently create something and it just happens that it's — it comes out identical or substantially identical, there's no copyright infringement there. What we're saying is that the white page listings information would not be here but for the telephone company gathering, collecting, and making a decision how to put this directory out and in fact each year it differs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Caplinger, this is to protect authors, isn't it?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: It is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Who' s the author of this?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Marshall, the telephone company.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, the telephone company.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That 's the author?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes. If it wasn't — if it wasn't for the telephone company, this directory wouldn't exist.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What individual. I never heard of a publisher being an author. An author's an individual, isn't he?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Marshall, an author could be a individual, a corporation, an entity who --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's usually the work of an individual, isn't it?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, customarily when you look at artwork —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The phone company couldn't exist without this, could they? Would you buy a phone if you didn't get a phone book?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: That's questionable, but —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, I just don't see all of the originality of somebody in this that just copied something out of a book— somebody's records.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, Justice Marshall, maybe I can — and I know this Court in getting prepared to argue here today, they would like to hear how not only the law would be affected by the facts of this case, but the industry nationwide. I think that if you went to New York City and you took NYNEX, who spent $2 million a year putting this information together.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As a matter of fact they charge you to take you name out.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I suppose you're going to have to do it alphabetically, because otherwise nobody can use it.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, Justice Stevens, I think that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't that right?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, we're told by the Kansas Corporation Commission to annually publish a directory. It doesn't say what's to be include in that directory.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you suppose any other form of directory would satisfy their requirement? , Have you ever seen a telephone directory of a different form than this one? That is, the white pages?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Stevens, yes. And in fact in subsequent years this directory is different. It goes back to what I was saying earlier —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In what respect does it differ?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Because of the — of this white page listing section could have been set out in 26 different alphabetical sections of the — encompassing the same listings because — in fact we have people that say that we're completely on the other side of western Kansas

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, I see what you're saying.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: — I would like to have you prepare your book to where you have the same listings, however, I'd like A through Z for Agra, A through Z for Bolta ■—

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. So that's your selection.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not the alphabetizing, it's if you had a single telephone company with just one area of service like the City of Chicago and nothing else, say, then it would have — there would -- the same telephone directory would have to be published year after year.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, and that would be a decision for them. I know it hard to stand here and try to say — and try to build something, because I don't believe the alphabet or alphabetizing something takes a genius of a great work of an author. But that's just one of a number of things that the author of this directory makes a decision on.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what other than the selection of which areas of service to be included in the directory would be different if we held it was not copyrightable? In fact, why would your decision be any different, whether it's copyrightable or not in your decision of whether to have one directory for all areas of service or several different directories? What difference does it make whether it's copyrighted or not?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: I'm not sure if I understand your question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: "Well, does the copyright law protection have any impact at all on your decision as to how to assemble the white pages?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Yes, Justice Stevens, the copyright law expresses our contribution by the author of creating this and those listings — how those listings are listed there —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand, but my question is would you create them any differently if thpre were no copyright law protection? And if so, why.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, Justice Stevens, to answer that question, there's a market for this information that if we do not put accurate, complete listing information, we're not going to sell the yellow page advertising.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well —

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: And in northwest Kansas, the revenue from this directory and from the yellow and white

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: From the yellow pages.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: -- goes back to — goes into the rate base to keep the rates or affect the rates to the subscribers and the owner-members of this coop. So you asked —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's true whether or not sell

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And how does that — I understand that it may intrude on your revenues from the advertising yellow pages, but otherwise it doesn't have any impact on your business at all, does it?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, we — there is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're trying to use your copyright on the white pages to protect your business — the business interest in the advertising in the yellow pages. That's what's at stake here, isn't it?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Well, there is revenue produced from the white page listings. We sell advertising in the white pages in the way of bold print. We get revenue from that. Extra listings we charge. So there is — the white pages are revenue producing on their own. But in reality you are correct. Without up-to-date accurate white page listings, the yellow pages — it would be difficult to maintain sales.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you — I take it the courts of appeals that have dealt with this are on your side.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Every — excuse me, Justice White, every single case since the landmark case of Leon in '37 to the most recent case of the United Telephone are — Illinois Bell —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the earliest case that's on your side?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Leon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What date was that?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: 1937. They call it the landmark telephone copyright case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And Congress didn't intend to change the definition of an original work of art in '76.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it they knew what the law was?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: That's -- Justice, White, that's exactly right and in fact —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does that bring us to Section 103? Could you spend a little time going through Section 103 with us, because I take it Feist's argument is that even if this is copyrightable, the information can still be taken subject to 103(b). Am I correct that that's the argument?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kenney, in my opinion of 103 was set out —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: I mean, Justice Kennedy. It was set out to show that the person who goes out and collects factual information just because he collects factual information and the work as a whole in a compilation is a protected work, that doesn't mean that you now own a copyright on those noncopyrightable bits of information, such as somebody's name. QUESTION; But isn't the problem under 103(b) and the problem for the sweat-of-the-brow theory that it provides that the protection extends only to the material contributed by the author and doesn't the word material imply something different from effort? Doesn't it imply something about the content of the work?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Justice Souter, my opinion as to what preexisting material means is preexisting fact —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, this — I'm not talking about preexisting material. I'm talking about the phrase that the copyright and the compilation of derivative work extends only to the material contributed by the author. What is the material contributed by the author? Doesn't that phrase imply that there is something about the content of the work rather than the process of compiling the work that is subject to protection? MR. CAPLINGER; Well, Justice Souter, I think you have to go back. 103 applies to two separate works. There's what is known as collective work, and that's what's at issue here, and then there's a derivative work. And a derivative work is where — and this might sound strange but since this is at issue — if we take this copyrighted telephone directory and make it into a movie. It's where you take a book and transform it to something else like a movie. They're saying that even though you change it, that the preexisting is still, you know, copyrighted or copyrightable. Maybe I'm not understanding your question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it's still -- unless I'm not parsing the sentence right, I think the sentence is still providing that the copyright in a compilation or derivative work, but in a compilation extends only to the material contributed by the author. And my, suggestion is that material implies something about the content of the work rather than something about the effort that goes into producing the work. My question is, number 1, is that a fair reading of the provision, and number 2, if it is, what is the material in your directory that is contributed by the author?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: Okay, Justice Souter, to answer that question is that the — again, the bits of factual information alone are not copyrightable. What the contribution is of the author in the form of collective work of a compilation is the hard work or labor as you put it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but that's not material.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: But the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's effort. It's thought, but

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: I'm not sure. I think section

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it was new, it could very well have had an effect on the extent to which the preexisting law, which we assume provides for copyright of such a directory, survived the amendment, wouldn't it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Caplinger, before you sit down, just as a matter of curiosity, what is the status of the antitrust aspect of this case?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: The antitrust casp —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any development yet?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: It is presently on appeal before the Tenth Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And they — has it been argued?

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: No. I filed Rural's opening brief a week ago and Mr. Knobbe's brief is due the first of March.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One last question. You come from Topeka, not northwestern Kansas. I don't know what they do in Topeka, but I would be surprised if the telephone company there didn't have a directory by street addresses. I've seen them.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: A city directory?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By street address, not alphabetically, and some of the questions here seem to assume that only an alphabetical arrangement is possible.

turns.name: JAMES M. CAPLINGER, JR.
turns.text: That's true, and you go back to the landmark case that I referred to earlier, Leon. That's where a second publisher took the alphabetized information and flipped it around and put it by telephone number and not A through Z. And the court said that that was still a taking of the work of a telephone company because they're the original author of that white page listing information. And also the most recent case, Illinois Bell —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think you've answered the question, Mr. Caplinger. Thank you. Mr. Knobbe, do you have rebuttal? You have 5 minutes remaining.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. I have short points. Perhaps when I answered your question, Mr. Chief Justice, that directories were copyrightable under the 1909 act but not under the '76 act, I misspoke. Our position is that facts have never been copyrightable. A point was made by Mr. —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All these decision against you were just wrong.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: They're wrong under this definition of the statute. Sweat of the brow has been rejected by the Second and the Ninth Circuit. The main cases cited, Leon out of the Ninth and Jeweler's Circular out of the Second, have both been disavowed by those very circuits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is Section 103(a) an addition to the 1976 act in 103(b)?

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: Yes, I think so, Justice Kennedy. The point was that the information here in this phone directory would not exist without — but for Rural Telephone was Mr. Caplinger's statement — and that's the problem with the independent canvas theory, is that you can't get it anywhere else except out of the phone directory. You can't canvas it. It's like the opinions of this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's very interesting, but I don't see how it has anything whatever to do with whether it's a copyrighted — I mean, that's a good argument for Congress.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: I'll pass over it then.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'll make an exception for this.

turns.name: KYLER KNOBBE
turns.text: The final point was that a copyright doesn't require inventiveness like a patent law, and I think that's correct, too. The copyright is a limited monopoly. It's not a patent on those facts, ideas, or public domain information. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Knobbe. The case is submitted.
